coredata.dc:title: Facile one-step synthesis of cerium oxide-carbon quantum dots/RGO nanohybrid catalyst and its enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Kannan, Ramanujam
Kim, Ae Rhan
Eo, Seong Kug
Kang, Seong Ho
Yoo, Dong Jin
coredata.dc:description: Improvement in the catalytic activity of nanostructured cerium oxides (CeOx) was attained by incorporating in-situ generated heteroatom-(N and S)-doped carbon quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide (HDCQD@RGO) nanohybrid catalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants. The CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst was synthesized by a facile, one-pot hydrothermal eco-friendly process. The HDCQD plays a vital role to improve the interaction between CeOx and RGO and function as a sensitizer for the electron-transfer process with CeOx. The size of CeOx and HDCQD are 10nm and 5nm, respectively. The nanostructured CeOx possesses multiple oxygen vacancies; this helps to generate the active oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. The active hydroxyl radical generation by the photocatalytic process with the help of nanostructured CeOx results in the improved photodegradation of organic pollutants. In addition, the prepared CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst exhibits an improved water oxidation reaction. The CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid performs as an excellent bifunctional catalyst for energy and environmental applications. The method presented here is a facile and environmentally friendly, scalable synthesis of the highly efficient catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructures
Oxide
Semiconductors
Photoluminescence spectroscopy
Heterostructures

Characterization of the synthesized catalytic materials
Surface characterizations of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst
Visible light catalytic degradation of dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM micrographs of (a) HDCD@RGO and (b and c) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
FE-SEM micrographs of (a) HDCD@RGO, (d)CeOx-HDCD@RGO, EDX mapping and profile (b, c) HDCD@RGO, and (e, f) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst,.
Powder XRD profile for (a) GO, (b) HDCD@RGO, (c) CeOx, and (d) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
Raman spectra for (a) GO, (b) HDCD@RGO, (c) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
Thermal stability for (a) GO, (b) RGO, (c) HDCD@RGO, and (d) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
XPS profile for CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
PL spectrum for (a) RGO, (b) CeOx-RGO, (c) HDCD@RGO, and (d) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
UV–Vis spectrum of (a) MB, adsorption of MB over (b) CeOx and (c) CeOx-RGO, (d) CeOx-HDCD@RGO, and (e) degradation of MB by CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst under visible light irradiation.
CVs for the water oxidation reaction by OER at (a) CeOx, (b) RGO, (c) RGO-CeOx, (d) CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst in 1.0M KOH solution (25mV/s).
Synthesis process of CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
Mechanism for the degradation of methylene blue dye under visible light and electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction occurred in CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst.
Proposed oxygen evolution through the electrochemical process in CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst; (Ov-Oxygen vacant site).

head.ce:title: Facile one-step synthesis of cerium oxide-carbon quantum dots/RGO nanohybrid catalyst and its enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Life Science, Chonbuk National University, Jeonju-si, Jeollabuk-do 54896, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Improvement in the catalytic activity of nanostructured cerium oxides (CeOx) was attained by incorporating in-situ generated heteroatom-(N and S)-doped carbon quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide (HDCQD@RGO) nanohybrid catalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants. The CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst was synthesized by a facile, one-pot hydrothermal eco-friendly process. The HDCQD plays a vital role to improve the interaction between CeOx and RGO and function as a sensitizer for the electron-transfer process with CeOx. The size of CeOx and HDCQD are 10nm and 5nm, respectively. The nanostructured CeOx possesses multiple oxygen vacancies; this helps to generate the active oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. The active hydroxyl radical generation by the photocatalytic process with the help of nanostructured CeOx results in the improved photodegradation of organic pollutants. In addition, the prepared CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst exhibits an improved water oxidation reaction. The CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid performs as an excellent bifunctional catalyst for energy and environmental applications. The method presented here is a facile and environmentally friendly, scalable synthesis of the highly efficient catalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis has the potential to enable utilization of the abundance of clean energy from natural sunlight for environmental remediation. The efficient degradation of organic pollutants is a critical problem which can be solved by a photochemical process such as an advanced oxidation process through the production of active oxygen using semiconductor materials [1–3].

Over the past two decades, nanostructured metal oxides have found extensive applications as catalysts in industrial processes, energy storage, and electrochemical sensors. Among various semiconducting materials, metal oxides received considerable research attention due to the high surface platform, improved mechanical, thermal and chemical stabilities, and excellent photochemical activities [1–3]. In a typical photocatalysis process, the semiconducting metal oxides absorbed light from the source, creating photogenerated electrons and holes. These photoproducts can be transferred to other molecules (pollutants), thereby successfully removing the pollutants [4].

Metal oxides, such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), tin oxide (SnO2), and cerium oxide (CeO2) exhibit exceptional photochemical activity [5–10]. The good photochemical activity of these metal oxides was used for hydrogen production through water splitting and/or decomposition of water pollutants. Among them, cerium oxide (ceria, CeO2−x where x=0 to 0.5) is one of the most widely used heterogeneous catalysts and an industrially important catalytic materials, due to higher oxygen storage capacity [10–12]. The compound has a higher oxygen storage capacity and redox ability (between Ce3+ and Ce4+ states finds extensive utilization in low–temperature water-gas shift reaction, oxygen sensors, oxygen permeation membrane systems and catalyst supports. On the other hand, the wide bandgap energy (Eg=3.19eV) and high dielectric constant (ε=24.5) [12] of ceria has attracted significant attention and extensive application in gas sensors, solid oxide fuel cells, optical devices, UV light blockers, and harmful pollutant filter in automobiles [10–16]. Recent research has been focused on the synthesis and catalytic activity of the nanoscale cerium oxide.

The widespread use of dye, about one million tons used annually, is extensively used in textile, paper, leather, petroleum, food, and cosmetics industry. contribute to environmental pollution and are potentially carcinogenic, the focus of recent efforts has been on removal of dyes from the environment. Several methods are available for dye/colorant removal from wastewater such as membrane separation, aerobic and anaerobic degradation using various microorganisms, chemical oxidation, coagulation and flocculation, adsorption and reverse osmosis [17,18]. Among these methods photocatalytic degradation is a particularly facile, cost-effective technology.

Due to the wide bandgap between conducting and valence bands, Cerium oxide is active in the UV region. Modifying the bandgap of the ceria will help to enhance the photophysical/chemical properties. Accordingly, researchers have focused on the development of visible light active ceria material by simple synthesis process. Recently, cobalt-doped ceria has shown improved photochemical activity in visible light for the degradation of organic pollutants [19]. Zirconia-ceria was utilized for the visible light degradation of 2,4-dicholorophenol and rhodamine B by Lin group [11]. However, the crystal structure may be disturbed with the incorporation of foreign elements. Thus, we attempted to improve the photochemical activity using a combination of ceria, carbon quantum dots and reduced graphene oxides for use as a nanohybrid catalyst.

Graphene has received much attention due to unique electronic properties; however, the intrinsic zero-bandgap properties limit its application in optoelectronics. Presently, efforts are being made to tune the bandgap to improve optoelectronic properties. Graphene and graphene-based carbon quantum dot materials exhibit good photophysical activity [2,3]. On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of nanostructured carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10nm and possessing excellent fluorescent/photoluminescent (PL) properties. Low-cost, abundance, chemical stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility have led to CDs extensive application in the biomedical, energy, and catalysis fields [3]. The PL from CDs can be quenched in solution by either an electron acceptor or an electron donor molecule, indicating photoexcited CDs are excellent donors and/or electron acceptors. This photoinduced electron transfer opens a novel platform for the development of photovoltaic materials. These photophysical/chemical properties of CDs were enhanced by heteroatoms such as sulfur and (Ed note: ok to insert the word <by>) nitrogen-doping [20]. Preparation and catalytic studies have been demonstrated by a number of authors, the sensitizing property of the CDs with other metal oxides have thus far not been studied. Thus, the electronic band of the ceria was engineered using in situ developed heteroatoms co-doped carbon dots by a simple one-pot, eco-friendly process.

Large scale energy storage produced from renewable energy sources has recently directed growing effects on the development of facilities and novel water electrolysis technologies [21–26]. In particular, alkaline water electrolysis shows a facile technique to generate hydrogen and oxygen. The efficiency of the electrolysis of water depends on the catalyst used, limited by the large anodic over the potential of the oxygen evolution. To overcome these, a catalyst was designed in such a way as to reduce the oxygen evolution potential. Recently, Liang et al. has studied the water oxidation by highly defective ceria supported Ruthenium oxide catalyst and suggested the defective structure of ceria helps improve the oxygen evolution process. However, the cost of the catalyst is also higher due to ruthenium oxide. In line with this, our recent reports show the RGO in the presence of a noble metal (palladium) and ceria exhibits improved oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [22,26,27]. However, we aim to reduce the cost of the catalyst, hence; non-precious metal based catalysts were developed for water oxidation.

This study focuses on the facile one-pot synthesis of cerium oxide@heteroatoms co-doped carbon dots over reduced graphene oxide (RGO) nanohybrid material a view towards its application in photocatalytic decomposition of organic dye and water oxidation. The results show that the prepared catalyst exhibits good photocatalytic degradation of dyes and good water electrolysis reaction. The details of the synthesis, characterizations, and the catalytic activities are discussed in detail.

Natural graphite powder was purchased from Alfa Aesar Chemicals. Methylene blue dye (MB, Color index: 52015), Methyl orange dye (MO, Color index: 13025) and L-cysteine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium permanganate, cerium nitrate, sulfuric acid, nitric acid and hydrogen peroxide were received from Samchun Pure Chemicals. All of the reagents were analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Double distilled, deionized water was used throughout the study.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using the modified Hummers’ method [25]. CeOx-RGO prepared by simple precipitation method. In brief, 10mg GO was dispersed with Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (1.6mmol, 5mL) and NaOH solution (1.4mmol, 35mL) and was mixed in a beaker. The resulting solution was kept at room temperature (21±2°C) and ambient pressure for 24h. The product was washed several times with distilled water and dried using domestic microwave oven [27].

The CeOx-HDCQD@RGO was prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal process. About 10mg of GO was mixed with Ce (NO3)3·6H2O solution (0.16mmol, 5mL) and 100mg L-cysteine was dispersed in 25mL of deionized water and subjected to ultrasonication for 5min. Then, the whole solution was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected hydrothermal reaction at 140°C for 12h. Then the resultant colloidal product/precipitate was washed with the deionized water by centrifugation at 20,000 RPM and dried in a hot air oven at 80°C for 24h. HDCQD@RGO was prepared by the same method described above without cerium precursor.

The catalytic studies were carried out by batch degradation method. Briefly, about 100mL of MB dye solution (Sigma-Aldrich, 0.1mmol) was taken in a 250mL conical flask and 50mg of different catalysts (RGO, CeOx-RGO, HDCQD@RGO, and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO) were added to the solution. The mixture was allowed to react at room temperature under visible light (white light, 500−600nm) conditions with continuous stirring. After 30min, a part of the solution was collected, centrifuged, diluted and was analyzed using double beam UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2500). About 100mL of the dye solution and 100mg of catalyst mixed together and subjected to photocatalytic reaction under visible light to evaluate the total organic carbon analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of electrode for the oxygen evolution studies
ce:section.ce:para: About 20mg of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO catalyst was dispersed ultrasonically in 0.1mL of a Nafion (0.05%)/0.9mL water solution; then, 10μL of the well-dispersed catalyst ink was dropped onto the glassy carbon (GC) electrode surface and vacuum dried at room temperature. The electrochemical studies were performed under nitrogen saturated condition at room temperature (23±2°C). Measurements were taken with respect to a standard calomel electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized catalytic materials
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO catalyst was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM 2100, JEOL), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FESEM, JEOL 6300 equipped with EDX, Oxford Instruments). The crystal structure of the catalytic material was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Instruments) of the powder. Raman spectra were obtained using a Nanofinder 30 from Tokyo Instruments (laser: 633nm). The electronic properties were obtained by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV 2500) and photoluminescence using thermal PL (JASCO 6500). The electrochemical properties of the catalysts were assessed on an electrochemical workstation (Biologic 320V) using a three-electrode system where the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO catalyst-modified glassy carbon, a platinum wire, and a saturated calomel electrode served as the working electrode, a counter electrode, and a reference electrode, respectively.

Extensive efforts have been made to develop carbon-based quantum dots (CDs) as they are non-toxic and possess excellent fluorescence properties compared to conventional semiconductor fluorescent materials, such as CdS or ZnS. Recent studies have demonstrated the effective CD synthesis by hydrothermal methods with good fluorescent properties and derived from a number of precursors. Specifically, L-Cysteine (LC) precursors exhibit good independent emission with higher quantum yield, due to the effective participation of heteroatoms such as N and S with the carbon dots [20]. Here we selected LC molecule because it possesses several advantages, such as performing as a reducing agent for converting graphene oxide (GO) into reduced graphene oxide (RGO), size controlling agent, the source for heteroatoms doped carbon dots (HDCQDs), and which is cost efficacy and eco-friendliness. Recently, the N- and S- doped CD formation is reported by Dong et al. [20], but the effective utilization of these HDCQD for catalytic applications has not been studied yet. Towards the effective utilization of the HDCQD for the practical application, we made an attempt to place HDCQD with the ceria and RGO. And we synthesize CeOx-HDCQD@RGO by a simple one-step hydrothermal method@RGO ( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface characterizations of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images are shown in Fig. 1. The HDCQD (Fig. 1a) and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO (Fig. 1b-d) show smaller particle size of 2–8nm. The fringe pattern of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO is displayed in Fig. 1c and d, and the distance between fringes is 0.4nm. Both the HDCQD and the ceria form spherical nanoparticles with diameters of 4–8nm. The high-resolution TEM images show the CeOx and HDCQD deposed over the RGO (Fig. S1, support information). The surface morphology and elemental analysis of the HDCQD@RGO and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO are tested using FESEM and EDX measurements; the results show that the Ce, O, N, and S are uniformly distributed over the catalyst. Both the EDX results the sulfur content is higher than the nitrogen ( Fig. 2).

The crystal structure of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid ( Fig. 3) catalyst was analyzed by XRD. Fig. 3a indicates the characteristic GO peak C (002) at 10.4°. HDCQD@RGO formation was observed in Fig. 3b, the C (002) peak of GO is suppressed and a new peak around (2θ) 23° is observed after the reaction of GO with LC, indicating the formation of the RGO. Some less intense peaks for carbon are observed in the XRD profile, ranging from (2θ) 18°–25°, and presumed to be the CD. Similar peaks with were also observed at CeOx-HDCQD@RGO (Fig. 3d). The ceria formed in the reaction is denoted as CeOx (due to the mixed valence cerium oxide, JCPDS 89-8436, Fig. 3c and d, based on XPS results).

The Raman spectrum of the as-prepared CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst showed three peaks at 451cm−1, 1345cm−1, and 1594cm−1 corresponding to F2g symmetry breathing mode of the oxygen atoms around the cerium cation, D-band, and G-band of the carbon material ( Fig. 4). The catalysts exhibit a shift of the F2g mode at a lower frequency, from 465cm−1 to 451cm−1. In addition to that the broadening of the peak at 600cm−1, associated with the smaller particle size with the defect structure [6]. The intensity of D and G bands ratios (ID/IG) are 1.04, 1.077, and 1.124 for GO, HDCQD@RGO, and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst, respectively. The Raman spectra for the RGO-CeOx exhibits a small change in the ID/IG value, whereas CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst shows a large difference in the ID/IG value compared to RGO, is due to the formation of HDCQD over the CeOx-RGO [25].

The thermal stability of the prepared materials, GO, HDCQD@RGO, and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalysts, are displayed in Fig. 5. The GO exhibits a gradual decrease in weight up to 200°C, and a sudden decrease in weight after 210°C due to the removal of oxygen functionalities. HDCQD@RGO and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO show two major stages of gradual degradation/weight loss. The weight loss at room temperature to 200°C is an attribute of the surface and intercalated water removed in the first stage and above 200°C the HDCQD was degraded. The HDCQD@RGO exhibits gradual degradation up to 400°C, whereas CeOx@HDCQD shows two distinguished degradations at 300°C and 600°C attributed to the degradation of HDCQD and CeOx transformation, with about 45–50% materials remaining at 800°C. The HDCQD@RGO and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO exhibit comparatively equal stability because of the incorporation of CeOx, and the as-prepared CeOx-HDCQD@RGO catalyst exhibits higher stability up to 600°C.

To investigate the chemical nature of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements of C1s, N1s, S2p, O1s, and Ce3d binding energies were carried out ( Fig. 6). The deconvoluted C1s of GO and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO are also shown in Fig. 6, with the GO exhibiting binding energy peaks at 284.3eV, 286.8eV, 288.8eV, and 289.6eV attributed to C−C/C˭C, C−O, C˭O, and C(O)O, respectively. However, the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO shows binding energies at 284.2eV, 285.6eV, and 288.2eV, corresponding to the C−C/C˭C, C−N/C−O, and C−NH2. The reduction in the C−O band indicates the effective reduction of GO in RGO occurred during the synthesis process, and the new C-N and C-NH2 peaks are due to the formation of HDCQD in the catalyst and was confirmed by the nitrogen analysis. The N1s exhibit peaks at 397.0eV, 399.1eV, and 404.5eV corresponds to the Ce−N, −C˭N and oxygenated nitrogen group (−NO2), respectively. The elemental analysis of sulfur (S2p) shows two characteristic peaks at 163.0eV and 164.6eV for C−S−C 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. Ce (Ce3d), core level spectra, exhibit signals of the very complicated Ce3d satellite structure. The Ce signals of 3d levels were labeled as v and u for spin-orbital coupling 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively. The binding energies of 881.0 (Ce3+), 900.1 (Ce3+), 901.6eV (Ce3+) indicate the Ce3+ ion and 882.0 (Ce4+), 883.1 (Ce4+), 885.7 (Ce4+), 889.0 (Ce4+), 909.6eV (Ce4+) represents the Ce4+ ion. The O1s exhibits three binding energy peaks at 529.0eV, 530.3eV and 532eV corresponding to the oxides and the hydroxide of Ce. These results indicate both Ce(III) and Ce(IV) oxidation states are presented in solid-state ceria in the catalyst. Hence, we represent CeOx [6,7].

To further investigate the optical properties, PL spectra of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst compared with the RGO, CeOx-RGO, and HDCQD@RGO are shown in Fig. 7. The GO exhibits a dominant emission peak at 432nm (excited at 270nm), whereas the intensity of the PL spectra is considerably reduced after combining the CeOx over the GO. Similarly, HDCQD@RGO sample also shows comparatively lower PL intensity than the GO and RGO-CeOx catalyst. The photoinduced electron transfer from metal oxide conduction band to RGO effectively separates the photoinduced electron transfer process in the graphene/metal oxide composite, useful for the photocatalytic applications. Similarly, the HDCQD also helps the electron transfer process. The combined and/or synergistic effects of HDCQD and RGO with CeOx, results of the low PL intensity of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid composite [7,28–30]. This lower PL intensity is ascribed to effective electron trapping by the CeOx with the help of HDCQD and RGO. Thus, it can be used for the photocatalytic application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible light catalytic degradation of dye
ce:section.ce:para: Recent review explained in details about the uses of CDs and discussed the photophysical properties. Sun et al. have reported that the CDs enhance the photosensitization of TiO2 when combined 2.7 times higher than pure TiO2. Based the previous reports we proposed that the photocatalytic activity of CeOx is improved by CDs [31]. The catalytic performance of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst on the decolourization of organic pollutants such as MB and MO dyes was studied. Fig. 8 shows the UV–visible spectra of the catalytic decomposition of MB with various catalysts such as CeOx, CeOx-RGO, HDCQD@RGO, and CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst (Fig. 8). The MB absorbance for the initial concentration of 0.1mM was recorded, and shows two characteristic peaks at 664nm and 620nm, corresponding to the monomer and dimer of the MB dye molecules. In order to find the catalytic efficiency, three different experiments were carried out as follows: (1) MB dye without the catalyst was irradiated with visible light, (2) MB dye and catalysts without visible light irradiation, and (3) MB dye and catalysts with visible light irradiation.

The first experiment was carried out in order to check the efficacy of visible light radiation on the decolourization of the dye and shows no appreciable change in optical density. Then, studies performed in the absence and presence of visible light irradiation showed a significant change in the absorbance. But in the absence of light, the dye color reappeared after 2h, whereas no obvious change was observed in systems subject to visible light irradiation. In the absence of light, the dye molecule absorbs more than the catalysts, and dye molecules degraded in the presence of visible light. Total organic carbon analysis indicates about 81% of MB decomposed under visible light irradiation in the presence of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO. This enhanced catalytic degradation of dye is attributed to the effective electron trapping by the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst. The electron excitation in CeOx from the valence band to the conduction band requires more energy (Eg=3.19eV), for this process the HDCQD and RGO help to trap the required energy from the visible.

The proposed mechanism for the MB/MO dye degradation on the surface of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst is shown in Scheme 2. In addition to that, the photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange dye also tested and the results were presented in the support information.

The photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange dye in the presence of CeOx-HDCQD@RGO was tested. The UV–Vis spectrum for the different experimental process and their results exhibits almost similar activity like MB dye. But the maximum degradation of MO dye observed at 60min. The catalyst recyclability is stable until the fifth cycle (Figs. S2 to S6).

The photocatalytic reactions on the surface of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst are explained based on the electron–hole mechanism: the separation of the electron and hole from the CeOx, formation of free radicals, and oxidation of the free radicals. The reaction is initiated when visible light irradiating the CeOx causes an electron from the conduction band and a hole from the valance band to diffuse to the CeOx in combination with the H2O, O2, and OH- generation of an active oxygen molecule (Scheme 2) [4]. The generated active oxygen molecule is a free radical and possesses the strong oxidizing ability, thereby effectively degrading the dye molecule. In the above process, the CDs helps to enhance the electron transferability [32].

Furthermore, the catalytic performance towards anodic water oxidation through the oxygen evolution was tested by cyclic voltammetry method with various catalyst modified glassy carbon electrodes. Fig. 9 shows the oxygen evolution voltammogram for CeOx, RGO, CeOx-RGO, and CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst modified electrodes in aqueous alkaline electrolyte medium (1M KOH) at 25mV/s (Fig. 9). The OER current for CeOx, RGO, RGO-CeOx and CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst at 1.0V are 2.8mA/cm2, 3.2mA/cm2, 9.6mA/cm2 and 38mA/cm2, respectively, in addition to the onset potential of the CeOx-HDCD@RGO catalyst exhibiting earlier (~+0.42V) compared to other electrodes. In addition, the CeOx-HDCD@RGO catalyst exhibits good oxygen reduction catalyst, which is observed between at −0.2V and −0.4V in the reverse scan. The improved catalytic ability of CeOx-HDCD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst is understood besides two factors: (i) small particle sized CeOx possess higher oxygen vacancies, which help to extract oxygen from the electrolyte [26] and (ii) the improved synergism between the RGO and CeOx by the HDCD results in enhanced oxygen evolution ( Scheme 3).
(1) Anode: 4 OH¯→ 2H2O + 4 e¯ + O2 ↑
(2) Cathode: 4 e¯ + 4H2O→ 4 OH¯ + 2H2 ↑

The proposed material exhibits good multifunctional catalytic properties toward energy (good oxygen evolution catalyst) and environment (photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant) applications. Further, other various carbon supported metal oxides nanostructures and possible ways to improve the interactions towards electrocatalysis especially OER performance is under progress.

Here, we demonstrate the facile, one-step, and synchronized synthesis of the CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst, via an eco-friendly process using L-cysteine as a reducing and stabilizing agent. The cerium nanoparticles and heteroatoms, such as sulfur and nitrogen, co-doped carbon dots with reduced graphene are generated by a single-step process. As a whole, the as-prepared CeOx-HDCQD@RGO nanohybrid catalyst is a novel bifunctional catalyst, which exhibits good photocatalytic activity towards organic dye decomposition and performed as an effective electrocatalyst for excellent OER with a maximum peak current in alkaline medium. The present procedure is a simple, green, and eco-friendly method for synthesis of the metal oxide nanoparticle-decorated carbon catalyst on a large-scale for energy and environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Korea Research Fellowship Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2015H1D3A1062507). This work was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) and the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (No. 20164030201070).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ceramint.2016.11.116.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
